_metadata.description: Augmentation vs. automation behavioral data for the Live Data Dashboard (Component 4A). Contains the aggregate behavioral split, five-pattern collaboration taxonomy, occupation-level usage shares, depth of AI usage across occupations, job zone analysis, and the self-report vs. behavioral comparison. Structured for Practice Activity P3 (Dashboard Exploration: Augmentation vs. Automation).

primary_source.title: Which Economic Tasks are Performed with AI? Evidence from Millions of Claude Conversations
primary_source.authors: Handa, K., Tamkin, A., McCain, M., Huang, S., Durmus, E., et al.
primary_source.sample: 1 million Claude.ai Free and Pro conversations per primary analysis; 4 million+ across all analyses in the paper

supplementary_sources.title: Introducing Anthropic Interviewer: What 1,250 Professionals Told Us About Working with AI
supplementary_sources.used_for: Self-reported augmentation/automation split for behavioral comparison in P3

supplementary_sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index Report: Economic Primitives
supplementary_sources.used_for: Updated occupation usage shares by platform (Claude.ai vs. API), temporal trend context

notes: Per-occupation augmentation/automation splits are NOT available in the source data. Handa et al. provides only the aggregate 57/43 split and states that 'this ratio varies across occupations' without giving per-occupation ratios. The dashboard should present occupation-level data as usage shares, not per-occupation aug/auto splits.

The Anthropic Interviewer paper reports a slightly different behavioral split (47% augmentation / 49% automation) described as 'our latest analysis.' This differs from Handa et al.'s 57/43 because it uses a different dataset and time period. Handa et al. is the standalone citable empirical study. See process journal discrepancy note.

Occupation usage share percentages from Figure 3 are read from chart values. Minor reading imprecision is possible for mid-range bars. The top categories (Computer and Mathematical at 37.2%, Arts/Design at 10.3%) are labeled explicitly in the figure.

CROSS-FILE V1 CONSISTENCY: This file uses 57% augmentation from Handa et al. (the primary citable study). adoption-trends.json uses 56% from Economic Primitives' retrospective reporting of the same V1 dataset. Decision: keep 57% here for the pattern breakdown; the trend chart uses Economic Primitives' retrospective figures. Dashboard should note this in a tooltip if both views are visible simultaneously.

VALIDATION PASS 1 (April 27, 2026): All data points verified against source documents. One fix applied: Personal Care and Service overrepresentation_ratio corrected from 0.3 to 0.2 (0.5/2.0 = 0.25, rounds to 0.2). Four approximate platform_comparison occupation pairs (Life/Physical/Social Science, Business/Financial, Management, Architecture/Engineering) read from Figure 1.2 small multiples confirmed as verified.

VALIDATION PASS 2 (April 27, 2026): Source-first verification — extracted all data from source documents independently, then diffed against JSON. Two fixes applied: (1) Validation typical_tasks removed unsupported 'code review' (source p. 10 mentions only language translations); (2) occupation_usage_shares data_note corrected source reference from 'footnote 20, p. 20' to 'Appendix B.7, p. 21' for the 23% non-work finding.

VALIDATION PASS 3 (April 27, 2026): Final reconciliation — comprehensive structural, computational, and cross-reference audit. All checks passed: 8/8 sections present, all percentage sums verified (collaboration patterns 100.0%, aggregate split 100%, self-report 100%, platform splits 100%, job zone conv. 100.01% matching source rounding), all 22 overrepresentation ratios recomputed correctly, all 5 job zone representation ratios verified, monotonic occupation depth confirmed, internal cross-references consistent, all three prior fixes confirmed in place. File is production-ready.

definition.augmentation: AI enhances human capabilities through collaboration — the user actively participates in shaping, evaluating, or iterating on the output.
definition.automation: AI directly executes tasks with minimal human involvement — the user delegates and accepts output with limited interaction.

aggregate_split.caveat: Users may edit and adjust responses outside the chat window, suggesting the true proportion of augmentative conversations may be even higher.

categories.definition: Collaborative refinement process — the user and AI iterate on a task together.
categories.example: Let's draft a marketing strategy for our new product. ... Good start, but can we add some concrete metrics?
categories.typical_tasks: Front-end development, landing pages, professional communication, resumes and cover letters

categories.definition: Knowledge acquisition and understanding — the user asks the AI for information or explanations.
categories.example: Can you explain how neural networks work?
categories.typical_tasks: General education topics, health advice, embedded systems, subject-specific learning

categories.definition: Work verification and improvement — the user asks the AI to check their work and suggest improvements.
categories.example: I've written this SQL query to find duplicate customer records. Can you check if my logic is correct and suggest any improvements?

categories.definition: Complete task delegation with minimal interaction — the user gives a task and the AI completes it.
categories.example: Format this technical documentation in Markdown.
categories.typical_tasks: Writing and content generation, business email drafting, schoolwork-related tasks

categories.definition: Task completion guided by environmental feedback — the user repeatedly relays error states back to the AI.
categories.example: Here's my Python script for data analysis – it's giving an IndexError. Can you help fix it? ... Now I'm getting a different error...

self_report_comparison.description: The behavioral data from Handa et al. diverges from how professionals self-report their AI usage patterns. This comparison is the core insight for Practice Activity P3.

behavioral.method: Automated classification of 1M Claude.ai conversations using Clio

gap_analysis.interpretation: Professionals overestimate their augmentative use by 8 percentage points. Behavioral data shows more automation than self-reports suggest — users believe they are collaborating when behavioral patterns indicate they are delegating.

occupation_usage_shares.description: Distribution of Claude.ai conversations across O*NET occupation categories, compared to U.S. workforce distribution. Shows where AI usage is concentrated relative to the labor market.
occupation_usage_shares.data_note: Values for Computer and Mathematical (37.2%) and Arts/Design (10.3%) are labeled in the original figure. Other values are read from the chart and may have minor imprecision (±0.2pp). Category percentages are expressed as shares of all conversations ('all queries') and sum to approximately 93%. The original analysis found that non-work conversations comprise 23% of the dataset (Appendix B.7, p. 21). The sum exceeding the expected ~77% occupational share likely reflects conversations classified into multiple occupation categories and chart reading imprecision.

categories.occupation: Arts, Design, Entertainment, Sports, and Media
Building and Grounds Cleaning and Maintenance

occupation_depth.description: Cumulative distribution of how deeply AI has penetrated occupations — what fraction of each occupation's tasks show AI usage. Shows that AI integration is selective within most occupations, not comprehensive.

thresholds.source_note: Approximate, read from curve. Explicitly stated as '~57% of occupations using AI for at least 10% of their tasks' on p. 13.
Labeled on Figure 4. Example: Physical Therapists (25% of tasks).
Labeled on Figure 4. Example: Marketing Managers (50% of tasks).
thresholds.example_tasks_without_ai: Product specification consultation, trade show coordination

thresholds.source_note: Labeled on Figure 4. Example: Foreign Language and Literature Teachers (75% of tasks).
thresholds.example_tasks_with_ai: Collaborating on teaching issues, planning course content
thresholds.example_tasks_without_ai: Writing grant proposals, maintaining student records

occupation_depth.insight: Rather than completely automating entire job roles, present-day AI appears to be primarily used for specific tasks within occupations.

job_zone_analysis.description: AI usage by occupational preparation level (O*NET Job Zones). Shows that AI usage peaks in occupations requiring a bachelor's degree, not at the extremes.
job_zone_analysis.source_table: Table 2 (p. 24); example occupations named on p. 20

zones.label: Some Preparation Needed
zones.example_occupations: Customer service representatives, security guards, tellers

zones.label: Considerable Preparation Needed
zones.example_occupations: Real estate brokers, sales managers, database administrators

job_zone_analysis.insight: AI usage is highest in occupations requiring substantial but not elite-level specialization. Both low-preparation and highly specialized roles show lower usage, likely due to physical task requirements and regulatory barriers.

platform_comparison.description: Comparison of occupation usage distributions between Claude.ai (consumer) and 1P API (enterprise) traffic. From Economic Primitives V4, November 2025 data.

source_pages.figures: Figure 1.2, p. 8 (usage by SOC over time); Figure 1.3, p. 10 (collaboration mode over time)

categories.occupation: Arts, Design, Entertainment, Sports, and Media

api.note: API usage is overwhelmingly directive — 64% of interactions classified as directive, 75% as automation overall. Reflects programmatic, single-turn task delegation. Claude.ai directive share is 32% by comparison.
api.verification: Figure 1.3, p. 10 (automation/augmentation); narrative text, p. 26 (directive 64% vs. 32%)

platform_comparison.insight: API deployments concentrate on tasks amenable to systematic automation, while Claude.ai serves a broader range of use cases including learning, creative work, and personal assistance.
